([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˌmɒntoʊˈbɒn, ˌmoʊntoʊˈbɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) French: [[mɔ̃tobɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Montauban.oga "File:Fr-Montauban.oga"); [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"): *Montalban* [[muntalˈβa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")) is a [commune](/wiki/Commune_of_France "Commune of France") in the southern French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Tarn-et-Garonne](/wiki/Tarn-et-Garonne "Tarn-et-Garonne"). It is the capital of the department and lies 50 kilometres (31 mi) north of [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"). Montauban is the most populated town in Tarn-et-Garonne, and the sixth most populated in Occitanie behind Toulouse, Montpellier, Nîmes, Perpignan and Béziers. In 2023, there were 62,945 inhabitants, called *Montalbanais* in French. The town has been classified in the [French Towns and Lands of Art and History](/wiki/French_Towns_and_Lands_of_Art_and_History "French Towns and Lands of Art and History") network since 2015.

**Montauban** ([US](/wiki/American_English "American English"): [/ˌmɒntoʊˈbɒn, ˌmoʊntoʊˈbɒ̃/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) French: [[mɔ̃tobɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Montauban.oga "File:Fr-Montauban.oga"); [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"): *Montalban* [[muntalˈβa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Occitan "Help:IPA/Occitan")) is a [commune](/wiki/Commune_of_France "Commune of France") in the southern French [department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Tarn-et-Garonne](/wiki/Tarn-et-Garonne "Tarn-et-Garonne"). It is the capital of the department and lies 50 kilometres (31 mi) north of [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"). Montauban is the most populated town in Tarn-et-Garonne, and the sixth most populated in Occitanie behind Toulouse, Montpellier, Nîmes, Perpignan and Béziers. In 2023, there were 62,945 inhabitants, called *Montalbanais* in French. The town has been classified in the [French Towns and Lands of Art and History](/wiki/French_Towns_and_Lands_of_Art_and_History "French Towns and Lands of Art and History") network since 2015.

The town, built mainly of reddish brick, stands on the right bank of the [Tarn](/wiki/Tarn_(river) "Tarn (river)") at its confluence with the [Tescou](/wiki/Tescou "Tescou").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Montauban_-_La_Place_Nationale.jpg/250px-Montauban_-_La_Place_Nationale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montauban_-_La_Place_Nationale.jpg)

Place Nationale in Montauban

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Montauban_arcades_de_la_place_Nationale.jpg/250px-Montauban_arcades_de_la_place_Nationale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Montauban_arcades_de_la_place_Nationale.jpg)

Arcade at Place Nationale

Montauban is the second oldest (after [Mont-de-Marsan](/wiki/Mont-de-Marsan "Mont-de-Marsan")) of the *[bastides](/wiki/Bastides "Bastides")* of southern France. Its foundation dates from 1144 when Count [Alphonse Jourdain](/wiki/Alphonse_I_of_Toulouse "Alphonse I of Toulouse") of [Toulouse](/wiki/County_of_Toulouse "County of Toulouse"), granted it a liberal charter. The inhabitants were drawn chiefly from Montauriol, a village which had grown up around the neighbouring monastery of [St Théodard](/wiki/St_Th%C3%A9odard "St Théodard").

In the 13th century the town suffered much from the ravages of the [Albigensian war](/wiki/Albigensian_war "Albigensian war") and from the [Inquisition](/wiki/Inquisition "Inquisition"), but by 1317 it had recovered sufficiently to be chosen by [John XXII](/wiki/Pope_John_XXII "Pope John XXII") as the head of a [diocese](/wiki/Diocese_of_Montauban "Diocese of Montauban") of which the basilica of St Théodard became [the cathedral](/wiki/Montauban_Cathedral "Montauban Cathedral").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/Reddition_de_la_ville_de_Montauban_21_Aout_1629.jpg/250px-Reddition_de_la_ville_de_Montauban_21_Aout_1629.jpg)](/wiki/File:Reddition_de_la_ville_de_Montauban_21_Aout_1629.jpg)

*[Surrender of Montauban](/wiki/Surrender_of_Montauban "Surrender of Montauban")*, 21 August 1629. [Château de Richelieu](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Richelieu "Château de Richelieu").

In 1360, under the [Treaty of Brétigny](/wiki/Treaty_of_Br%C3%A9tigny "Treaty of Brétigny"), it was ceded to the English; they were expelled by the inhabitants in 1414. In 1560 the bishops and magistrates embraced Protestantism, expelled the monks, and demolished the cathedral. Ten years later it became one of the four [Huguenot](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot") strongholds under the [Peace of Saint-Germain](/wiki/Peace_of_Saint-Germain "Peace of Saint-Germain"), and formed a small independent republic. It was the headquarters of the [Huguenot rebellion](/wiki/Huguenot_rebellion "Huguenot rebellion") of 1621, and successfully withstood an 86-day siege by [Louis XIII](/wiki/Louis_XIII "Louis XIII").

Because Montauban was a Protestant town, it resisted and held its position against royal power, refusing to give allegiance to the Catholic King. To scare off the King's opponents and speed up the end of the siege, 400 cannonballs were fired, but Montauban resisted and the royal army was vanquished. Saint Jacques church is still marked by the cannonballs, and every year in September, the city celebrates "les 400 coups" (the 400 shots), which has become a common phrase in French.

Montauban did not submit to royal authority until after the fall of [La Rochelle](/wiki/La_Rochelle "La Rochelle") in 1629, when its [fortifications](/wiki/Fortification "Fortification") were destroyed by [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu"). The Protestants again suffered persecution later in the century, as [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV") began to persecute Protestants by sending troops to their homes ([dragonnades](/wiki/Dragonnades "Dragonnades")) and then in 1685 revoked the [Edict of Nantes](/wiki/Edict_of_Nantes "Edict of Nantes"), which had granted the community tolerance.

During World War II, Leonardo da Vinci's *[Mona Lisa](/wiki/Mona_Lisa "Mona Lisa")* was briefly hidden in a secret vault behind a wine cellar in Montauban.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Climate")]

Montauban has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfa*), with mild winters and hot summers. The town experienced severe droughts in 2003, 2006, 2012 and 2015. On 31 August 2015, the Tarn-et-Garonne area was particularly struck by a wave of violent storms. These storms, accompanied by very strong winds, created a tornado, which caused considerable damage in a large part of the department. Montauban was particularly affected, with winds measured between 130 and 150 kilometers per hour (a record) in the city center.

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Sights")]

Its fortifications have been replaced by boulevards beyond which extend numerous suburbs, while on the left bank of the Tarn is the suburb of [Villebourbon](/w/index.php?title=Villebourbon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Villebourbon (page does not exist)"), which is connected to the town by a remarkable bridge of the early 14th century. This bridge is known as *Pont Vieux* (i.e. "Old Bridge"). King [Philip the Fair](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_France "Philip IV of France") of France officially launched the building of the bridge in 1303 while on a tour to [Toulouse](/wiki/Toulouse "Toulouse"). The project took 30 years to complete, and the bridge was inaugurated in 1335. The main architects were [Étienne de Ferrières](/w/index.php?title=%C3%89tienne_de_Ferri%C3%A8res&action=edit&redlink=1 "Étienne de Ferrières (page does not exist)") and [Mathieu de Verdun](/w/index.php?title=Mathieu_de_Verdun&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mathieu de Verdun (page does not exist)"). It is a pink brick structure over 205 metres (224 yards) in length, but while its fortified towers have disappeared, it is otherwise in a good state of preservation. The bridge was designed to resist the violent floods of the [Tarn](/wiki/Tarn_(river) "Tarn (river)"), and indeed it successfully withstood the two terrible millennial floods of 1441 and 1930. The bridge is a straight level bridge, which is quite unusual for Medieval Europe, where lack of technological skills meant that most bridges were of the humpback type.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Bemberg_Fondation_Toulouse_-_The_Great_Warrior_of_Montauban_by_Antoine_Bourdelle.jpg/250px-Bemberg_Fondation_Toulouse_-_The_Great_Warrior_of_Montauban_by_Antoine_Bourdelle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bemberg_Fondation_Toulouse_-_The_Great_Warrior_of_Montauban_by_Antoine_Bourdelle.jpg)

*[The Great Warrior of Montauban](/wiki/The_Great_Warrior_of_Montauban "The Great Warrior of Montauban")* by [Antoine Bourdelle](/wiki/Antoine_Bourdelle "Antoine Bourdelle").

The *[Musée Ingres](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Ingres "Musée Ingres")*, on the site of the castle of the Counts of Toulouse and once the residence of the bishops of [Montauban](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Montauban "Bishopric of Montauban"), stands at the east end of the bridge. It belongs chiefly to the 17th century, but some portions are much older, notably an underground chamber known as the Hall of the [Black Prince](/wiki/Black_Prince "Black Prince") (*Salle du Prince Noir*). It comprises most of the work (including his "Jesus among the Teachers of the Law") of [Jean Ingres](/wiki/Jean_Auguste_Dominique_Ingres "Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres"), the celebrated painter, whose birth in Montauban is commemorated by an elaborate monument. It is the largest museum of Ingres paintings in the world. The museum also contains some sculptures by famous sculptor [Antoine Bourdelle](/wiki/Antoine_Bourdelle "Antoine Bourdelle"), another native of Montauban, as well as collections of antiquities (Greek vases) and 18th and 19th [ceramics](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery").

The *Place Nationale* is a square of the 17th century, entered at each corner by gateways giving access to a large open space surrounded by pink brick houses supported by double rows of arcades.

The *[préfecture](/wiki/Pr%C3%A9fecture "Préfecture")* is located in the palace built by the *intendant* of Montauban (the equivalent of a *[préfet](/wiki/Pr%C3%A9fet "Préfet")* before the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution")), and is a large elegant 18th century mansion, built of pink bricks and white stone, with a steep roof of blue gray [slates](/wiki/Slate "Slate"), in a style combining northern and southern French styles of architecture.

The chief churches of Montauban are [the cathedral](/wiki/Montauban_Cathedral "Montauban Cathedral"), remarkable only for the possession of the "Vow of Louis XIII", one of the masterpieces of Ingres, and the church of St Jacques (14th and 15th centuries), dedicated to [Saint James of Compostela](/wiki/Saint_James_the_Great "Saint James the Great"), the façade of which is surmounted by a handsome octagonal tower, the base of which is in [Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") style, while the upper levels, built later, are in [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") style.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Economy")]

The commercial importance of Montauban is due to its trade in agricultural produce, horses, game and poultry, than to its industries, which include nursery-gardening, cloth-weaving, cloth-dressing, flour-milling, wood-sawing, and the manufacture of furniture, silk-gauze and straw hats.

However, due to the proximity of Toulouse and the cheaper cost of industrial grounds, more and more mechanical products are being manufactured there.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transport")]

The town is a railway junction, and the station [Gare de Montauban-Ville-Bourbon](/wiki/Gare_de_Montauban-Ville-Bourbon "Gare de Montauban-Ville-Bourbon") offers connections with Toulouse, Bordeaux, Paris, Brive-la-Gaillarde, Marseille and several regional destinations. Montauban communicates with the [Garonne](/wiki/Garonne "Garonne") via the [Canal de Montech](/wiki/Canal_de_Montech "Canal de Montech"). The nearest airport is [Toulouse–Blagnac Airport](/wiki/Toulouse%E2%80%93Blagnac_Airport "Toulouse–Blagnac Airport"), located 54 km (34 mi) south of Montauban.

Founded in 1144 by the Comte de Toulouse, the town of Montauban has some particularities: its center's red brick streets intersect at right angles and meet at the National Square (Place Nationale) which is ranked among the most beautiful squares of France. Some buildings and architectural complexes are distinguished, such as "le [Musée Ingres](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Ingres "Musée Ingres")", "la Place Nationale", "le Pont vieux", "L’église Saint Jacques", "[la Cathédrale Notre Dame](/wiki/Montauban_Cathedral "Montauban Cathedral")", «  l’Ancien Collège des Jésuites », « le Muséum ».

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Main sights")]

* The [Ingres-Bourdelle Museum](/wiki/Mus%C3%A9e_Ingres_Bourdelle "Musée Ingres Bourdelle") is the old town hall and an [episcopal](/wiki/Episcopium "Episcopium") palace built in 1664 at the initiative of Pierre de Bertier on the remains of the palace that the [Black Prince](/wiki/Edward_the_Black_Prince "Edward the Black Prince") occupied during the [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War"). Some of the rooms of the latter, in the basements, are open to visitors. The building houses works by two former residents: [Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres](/wiki/Jean-Auguste-Dominique_Ingres "Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres") and [Antoine Bourdelle](/wiki/Antoine_Bourdelle "Antoine Bourdelle").[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* The fortified church of Saint-Jacques. Of the second church built in the XIII-th century, only the Toulouse-style bell tower and part of the nave remain.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In the XIVth century, the flat apse was replaced by a polygonal apse, while the city was going through a period of prosperity and the church became the seat of a parish. Transformed into a watchtower (bell tower), saltpeter manufacturing workshop (nave) and fort (choir) during the [French Wars of Religion](/wiki/French_Wars_of_Religion "French Wars of Religion"), Saint-Jacques still bears traces of cannonballs from the siege of 1621 on its facade. After the Catholic reconquest (1629), [Cardinal Richelieu](/wiki/Cardinal_Richelieu "Cardinal Richelieu") ordered the identical reconstruction of the church. Once a cathedral (1629–1739), in the 18th century it was equipped with new side portals and a gallery. On the facade, the [Neo-Romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival_architecture "Romanesque Revival architecture") portal topped with a mosaic dates from the XIX-th century.
* The [Hôtel de Ville](/wiki/H%C3%B4tel_de_Ville,_Montauban "Hôtel de Ville, Montauban") (town hall) was commissioned as a private house and completed c. 1695.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sport")]

The town is home of the [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union") club [US Montauban](/wiki/US_Montauban "US Montauban"). The team gained promotion from the Pro D2 competition for the 2006–07 [Top 14](/wiki/Top_14 "Top 14") season. The whole town supports rugby, but the athletic club is also very efficient and national results have been regular since 2007. Some athletes in Montauban's athletic club are international athletes. Every year, since 2004, the Rene Arcuset cross country race has been organized in the city.

## Movies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Movies")]

In the movie *[Les Tontons Flingueurs](/wiki/Les_Tontons_Flingueurs "Les Tontons Flingueurs")* a French classic by [Georges Lautner](/wiki/Georges_Lautner "Georges Lautner"), shot and released in 1963, [Lino Ventura](/wiki/Lino_Ventura "Lino Ventura")'s character is a businessman from Montauban. Called to Paris for a personal case, he is nicknamed by [Bernard Blier](/wiki/Bernard_Blier "Bernard Blier")'s character "Le gugusse de Montauban" (the guy from Montauban.) The "gugusse" will later answer: "one should never leave Montauban". Recently, a round-about in the center of the town was renamed "Tonton Flingueurs' round-about" and placards with drawings of the actors have been displayed.

## Institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Montauban&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Institutions")]

Montauban is the seat of a bishop and a court of assize. It has tribunals of first instance and of commerce, a chamber of commerce and a board of trade arbitration, lycées and a training college, schools of commerce and viticulture, a branch of the Bank of France, and a faculty of [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology").
